concept_ref: §3 row 2 — 'Statement coverage guarantees branch coverage' (corrected by Branch testing)

scenario.title: The Empty Loop
scenario.narrative: A batch processor walks through a list of items. A `for` loop has a TRUE edge (items present, body runs) and a FALSE edge (empty list, body skipped). Pick the candidate test set that achieves 100% BRANCH coverage of the loop guard.

conditions.label: items is non-empty (loop body executed at least once)

claim: Which candidate test set achieves 100% BRANCH coverage of the loop guard in process_all()?
charges: The batch processor process_all(items) iterates over a list and counts handled items inside the loop body.
The defendant test suite exercises only the case where items is non-empty, so the loop body always runs.
The prosecution alleges the FALSE edge of the for-loop guard (empty list, body skipped) is never taken, leaving an empty-input fault undetectable.

options.label: Set A — { items=[1,2,3] }
options.explanation: Statement coverage only. The loop body runs (TRUE edge), but the FALSE edge (empty list, body skipped) is never exercised. A bug on the empty-list path stays hidden.

options.label: Set B — { items=[1,2,3], items=['x'] }
options.explanation: Both tests take the SAME loop edge (TRUE). Adding more non-empty inputs doesn't help — Branch coverage requires a different edge of the predicate, not more variety on the same edge.

options.label: Set C — { items=[1,2,3], items=[] }
options.explanation: Correct. The non-empty list takes the TRUE edge of the `for` guard; the empty list takes the FALSE edge (body skipped). Both edges are exercised — Branch coverage is 100%.

options.label: Set D — { items=[] }
options.explanation: Only the FALSE edge of the loop is taken. The TRUE edge (loop body running) is never reached, so Statement coverage drops to ~50% and Branch is also incomplete.

correct_answer_explanation: Branch coverage of a loop requires BOTH a non-empty input (body executes) AND an empty input (body skipped). One alone leaves the other edge untested.
wrong_answer_explanation: A `for` loop has two edges: enter the body, skip the body. Branch coverage demands both.
hints: A `for` (or `while`) loop has a TRUE edge (enter the body) and a FALSE edge (skip the body / exit immediately). Branch coverage demands tests for both edges.
T1=[1,2,3] enters the loop body — so the TRUE edge is covered. Which test set ALSO covers the FALSE edge?
Set C is the only candidate that includes both items=[non-empty] (TRUE edge) AND items=[] (FALSE edge). That's 100% Branch coverage.

seeded_faults.description: process_all returns the wrong default (e.g. None) when items is empty.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Statement coverage does NOT guarantee Branch coverage. A loop that is entered at least once executes every line, satisfying Statement testing — but the implicit 'skip the loop' edge stays untested. To detect empty-list bugs you must add a test that exercises the FALSE edge of the loop predicate.
